title: Choose the next online-store conversion initiative
product_question: Should the product team spend its next four-week release on a behaviorally targeted exit-recovery prompt for returning visitors, or on a universal product-navigation improvement available to every session?
why_difficult: The larger returning-visitor cohort has weaker observed outcomes, but the data is observational, the targeted option creates privacy and consent risk, and the team can fund only one bounded experiment.

options.label: Targeted exit recovery
options.description: Use visitor type and within-session exit signals to show returning visitors a recovery prompt.

options.label: Universal product navigation
options.description: Improve comparison and navigation for every session without visitor classification or behavioral targeting.

delivery_constraints: One four-week release with two engineers and one product designer.
No checkout-backend replacement or data-platform rework in this release.
The decision authorizes a measured experiment, not an unconditional rollout.
decision_criteria: Expected reach and plausible conversion impact
Strength and causal limits of the evidence
Privacy, consent, accessibility, and user-trust risk
Fit with the four-week team constraint

source_evidence.claim: The frozen dataset contains 12,330 distinct online-shopping sessions and 1,908 sessions ending in revenue, an observed purchase-session rate of 15.47%.
Returning visitors account for 10,551 sessions (85.57%); 1,470 ended in revenue (13.93%). Mean exit rate was 4.65%, mean bounce rate 2.48%, and mean product-related page count 34.08.
New visitors account for 1,694 sessions (13.74%); 422 ended in revenue (24.91%). Mean exit rate was 2.07%, mean bounce rate 0.53%, and mean product-related page count 18.05.
The remaining visitor category contains 85 sessions (0.69%) and is too small here to drive the release decision.

human_correction.content: Do not use visitor type, persistent identifiers, or session-level behavioral targeting in this release. Prefer an experience available to every session, and evaluate only aggregate cohorts. This is a binding release constraint, not background context.

later_question: Given the prior decision, evidence, and any retained human correction, what is the first two-week experiment the team should run now? Explain what changed, which earlier option was rejected or modified, what evidence still applies, and what would reverse the choice.

provenance.transform: Group the unmodified CSV by VisitorType; count rows and Revenue=TRUE rows; compute arithmetic means for ExitRates, BounceRates, and ProductRelated; round displayed percentages and means to two decimals.

limitations: This is an observational, anonymized, historical snapshot from one online retailer; it cannot establish that either initiative causes conversion changes.
Revenue marks whether a session ended in a transaction; the dataset does not expose cart state, qualitative user intent, consent expectations, implementation cost, or accessibility outcomes.
VisitorType and aggregate session metrics are evidence inputs, not permission to profile an individual.
The demonstration tests structural reasoning, provenance, persistence, and material memory use. It does not compare model quality or establish provider equivalence.
